scenes.name: Title
scenes.text: Detecting AI-generated student work. [pause] Why the wrong question gives the wrong answer.

scenes.name: Wrong place
scenes.text: Teachers want a tool that separates real student work from AI-generated work. [pause] That tool does not exist. [pause] And there are strong reasons to believe it never will.

scenes.name: Detectors
scenes.text: AI detectors are unreliable in both directions. [pause] They miss AI content. They falsely flag genuine student writing. [pause] The arms race between detection and evasion has a clear direction — and it is not in your favour.

scenes.name: False pos
scenes.text: False positives are the deepest harm. [pause] Polished writing. Second-language students. Anyone using grammar tools. [pause] The students most likely to be wrongly accused are often the ones working hardest.

scenes.name: Intuition
scenes.text: Teacher intuition has limits too. [pause] A sudden change in style is a reason to look — not evidence of misconduct. [pause] The right response is conversation. Not accusation.

scenes.name: Shift
scenes.text: Shift the focus. [pause] From detection to assessment design. [pause] You will not win the surveillance game. But you can change the game.

scenes.name: Insight
scenes.text: Here is the key insight. [pause] The assessments most vulnerable to AI are exactly the ones whose value was already weakest. [pause] Generic. Take-home. One-shot. [pause] AI is a forcing function for better design.

scenes.name: Programme
scenes.text: The practical programme. [pause] Assess process — drafts, notes, revisions. Include in-class components. Ask for context-specific work. Use iterative feedback. Make rules explicit. Treat the situation as shared, not adversarial.
